walkthrough.title: Unbox and Organize
walkthrough.text: Let's open up Sushi Go Party! and take a look at everything inside. We have one hundred eighty-one cards, twenty-two menu tiles, eight colored pawns, and a game board with a score track. Sort the cards by type using the colored borders on the backs.

walkthrough.title: Place the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's put the game board in the center of the table where everyone can see it. The board has slots for menu tiles along the top and a score track around the edges. This board is the heart of Sushi Go Party!

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Menu
walkthrough.text: Now let's build our menu. Pick a preset menu from the rulebook or create a custom one. A legal menu needs nigiri, one roll type, three appetizer types, two special types, and one dessert type. For your first game, use the "My First Meal" preset.

walkthrough.title: Place Menu Tiles
walkthrough.text: Let's place the matching menu tiles into the board slots. These tiles show the scoring rules for each card type in your chosen menu. If you are playing with six to eight players, flip the maki roll tile to the six to eight player side.

walkthrough.title: Build the Deck and Dessert Pile
walkthrough.text: Let's gather all the cards for our chosen menu types and shuffle them into one face-down deck. Separate out the chosen dessert cards into their own face-down pile. We will add desserts to the main deck at the start of each round.

walkthrough.title: Player Setup
walkthrough.text: Each player chooses a colored pawn and places it on or near zero on the score track. Keep your played cards in front of you during the game, grouped by type so scoring is easy to see at a glance.

walkthrough.title: Add Desserts and Deal
walkthrough.text: Before round one, add dessert cards to the main deck. With two to five players, add five desserts. With six to eight players, add seven. Shuffle, then deal cards based on player count: ten cards for two to three players, nine for four to five, eight for six to seven, and seven for eight.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Let's confirm everything is set. Menu tiles are on the board, pawns are at zero, desserts are shuffled in, and everyone has their hand of cards. Keep remaining cards face down as a draw pile. We are ready for our sushi feast!

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how Sushi Go Party! works. This is a card-drafting game where everyone plays simultaneously over three rounds. Each game feels different because we choose which card types are on the menu before playing.

walkthrough.title: Pick and Reveal
walkthrough.text: Everyone secretly chooses one card from their hand and places it face down. Once all players are ready, everyone reveals their chosen card at the same time. Place the card face up in your tableau, grouped by type.

walkthrough.title: Pass Your Hand
walkthrough.text: After revealing, pass your remaining cards face down to the player on your left. Pick up the hand from your right. We keep picking, revealing, and passing until all cards are played. That completes one round.

walkthrough.title: Resolve Special Effects
walkthrough.text: Some cards have immediate effects when revealed. If multiple effects trigger on the same turn, resolve them by the small printed priority numbers, lowest first. Miso Soup, for example, is discarded if any other player also plays Miso Soup on the same turn.

walkthrough.title: Nigiri and Wasabi
walkthrough.text: Nigiri cards score on their own: egg is one point, salmon is two, squid is three. Wasabi triples the very next nigiri you play on it. The wasabi stays face up waiting for a nigiri to land on it.

walkthrough.title: Roll Types
walkthrough.text: Your menu includes one of three roll types. Maki rolls score by majority of roll icons at round end. Temaki scores the most icons at round end and penalizes the fewest. Uramaki is a race where you score immediately when your icons reach ten or more.

walkthrough.title: Appetizer Scoring
walkthrough.text: Appetizers score by their individual card rules. Tempura needs pairs for five points. Sashimi needs triples for ten. Dumplings scale with quantity. Other appetizers like eel, tofu, onigiri, edamame, and miso soup each have their own unique scoring pattern shown on the menu tile.

walkthrough.title: Special Cards
walkthrough.text: Special cards modify gameplay. Chopsticks let you play two cards on a future turn. Spoon lets you request a specific card from an opponent. Menu lets you draw four cards and play one. Soy Sauce, Tea, Special Order, and Takeout Box each offer unique scoring bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Round Scoring
walkthrough.text: When all hands are empty, score everything except desserts. Add points to your pawn on the score track. Move dessert cards to a personal side area. Discard all other played cards back into the deck.

walkthrough.title: Between Rounds
walkthrough.text: Before rounds two and three, add more dessert cards to the deck. With two to five players, add three desserts for round two and two for round three. With six to eight players, add five and three respectively. Shuffle and deal new hands.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Differences
walkthrough.text: The game adjusts by player count. With two to five players, maki scoring is six and three. With six to eight, it is six, four, and two for three places. In two-player games, temaki has no penalty for fewest. Spoon and edamame cannot be used with two players. Menu and special order cannot be used with seven or eight.

walkthrough.title: End of Game
walkthrough.text: After three rounds, score the dessert cards. Pudding awards six points for most and penalizes six for fewest. Green tea ice cream scores twelve for sets of four. Fruit scores based on type variety. Add dessert points to your total. Highest score wins, with most dessert cards breaking ties.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Party
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Sushi Go Party! We will walk through a practice round with four players using the "My First Meal" preset menu: nigiri, maki rolls, tempura, sashimi, miso soup, wasabi, tea, and green tea ice cream. Each player has nine cards.

walkthrough.title: Check the Menu Tiles
walkthrough.text: Before we draft, let's look at the menu tiles on the board. Each tile shows exactly how its card type scores. The maki tile shows six points for first and three for second. The tempura tile shows a pair equals five. These tiles are your scoring reference all game.

walkthrough.title: First Pick
walkthrough.text: Everyone looks at their hand. Player one sees wasabi and takes it, hoping for a squid nigiri. Player two grabs a maki roll with three icons. Player three takes miso soup. Player four takes a green tea ice cream to start their dessert collection.

walkthrough.title: Passing and Second Pick
walkthrough.text: Everyone passes left. Player one receives a hand with squid nigiri and plays it on their wasabi for nine points. Player two takes another maki roll. Player three takes a tempura. Player four takes a sashimi to start a set.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Miso Soup Clash
walkthrough.text: On the third pick, Player three plays another miso soup. But Player one also plays a miso soup from their hand. When two miso soups are revealed on the same turn, both are discarded and score zero. Player three just wasted a pick.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Miso Soup Timing
walkthrough.text: Miso soup is risky because it gets discarded if any other player plays one at the same time. It is safest to play miso soup late in the round when fewer copies remain in circulation. Early in the round, the collision risk is too high.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Round Action
walkthrough.text: The draft continues. Player two now has five maki icons and is leading the maki race. Player four picks up a second green tea ice cream. Player one grabs a tea card, which will score one point for each card in their largest set. Player three pivots to tempura.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Ignoring the Menu Tiles
walkthrough.text: Player four has been collecting sashimi but forgot to check the menu tile. They assumed sashimi works like the original Sushi Go!, but in Party the tile might show different details. Always reference the menu tiles because scoring can vary between menus.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Read the Tiles
walkthrough.text: The menu tiles are your scoring bible for the entire game. Every card type scores exactly as shown on its tile. Before committing to a strategy, check the tile to confirm points, set sizes, and special conditions. Do not assume you know the rules from memory.

walkthrough.title: Late Round Picks
walkthrough.text: With only three cards left per hand, options are thin. Player three completes their tempura pair for five points. Player two takes a final maki roll to lock in six icons. Player one grabs a dumpling. Player four takes one more green tea ice cream, reaching three of the four they need.

walkthrough.title: Desserts Move Aside
walkthrough.text: Let's score the round. Everyone scores their non-dessert cards and advances their pawn. Then we move all dessert cards to each player's personal side area. Player four sets aside their three green tea ice cream cards. These will be scored at the very end of the game.

walkthrough.title: A Beginner Mistake: Forgetting Dessert Adds
walkthrough.text: Before dealing round two, we must add more dessert cards to the deck. Player three almost forgot this step. With four players, we add three more dessert cards to the main deck and shuffle. Skipping this step means fewer desserts in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Follow the Round Prep Steps
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, always add the correct number of dessert cards before dealing. The amounts change each round and by player count. Check the rulebook or menu tile for the exact numbers. Missing this step throws off the entire dessert economy.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Round
walkthrough.text: Let's tally up. Player one scored nine from wasabi plus squid nigiri and two from their tea card for eleven total. Player two scored six from maki first place. Player three got five from tempura but lost a pick to the miso soup collision. Player four scored three from sashimi but has great dessert potential.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first practice round of Sushi Go Party! You learned about miso soup collision risk, the importance of reading menu tiles, and the dessert-adding step between rounds. These details make Party deeper than the original.

walkthrough.title: Ready for the Full Feast
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for menu selection advice and drafting strategies.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick list of all card types and player-count rules.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Learn the Preset Menus First
walkthrough.text: Start with the preset menus before building custom ones. Each preset is designed to create a balanced experience. "My First Meal" is great for beginners, while "Master Menu" adds complexity. Play several presets before experimenting with custom combinations.

walkthrough.title: Commit to a Lane Early
walkthrough.text: In Sushi Go Party! there are many scoring options, but trying to collect everything leads to mediocre results. Pick one or two main scoring lanes in your first few picks and commit. Partial sets of tempura and sashimi are worth zero, so focused play wins.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Roll Race
walkthrough.text: Rolls are a guaranteed scoring category in every game. Whether it is maki, temaki, or uramaki, someone will contest you. Track how many roll icons your neighbors are accumulating and decide early if you want to compete or concede that category.

walkthrough.title: Plan Your Dessert Strategy
walkthrough.text: Desserts only score at the end of the game, but they can swing the final standings by twelve or more points. Take at least one dessert per round so you are not caught empty-handed. Green tea ice cream needs exactly four for its full twelve-point bonus.

walkthrough.title: Use Specials Wisely
walkthrough.text: Special cards like chopsticks and spoon are powerful but require setup. Take chopsticks early when you have many turns left to use them. Spoon is best when you know exactly what card you need and opponents likely have it. Do not waste specials on low-value plays.

walkthrough.title: Adapt to Player Count
walkthrough.text: The game feels very different at two players versus eight players. With fewer players, you see more of the deck and can plan ahead. With more players, the draft is chaotic and sets are harder to complete. Adjust your risk tolerance for large sets based on how many players are competing.

walkthrough.title: Read the Table
walkthrough.text: Everyone's played cards are face up. Check what your neighbors are collecting to predict what they will take from passing hands. If the player on your right is hoarding dumplings, do not expect many to reach you. Adapt your strategy to the information you can see.

walkthrough.title: Denial Drafting
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best pick is not the best card for you but the worst card for an opponent. If someone needs one more sashimi for ten points, taking it denies them a huge score. Denial picks are especially valuable late in a round when sets are nearly complete.

walkthrough.title: Menu Construction Theory
walkthrough.text: When building custom menus, think about how card types interact. Wasabi pairs with nigiri. Soy sauce rewards diverse collections. Tea rewards depth in one type. Choose combinations that create interesting decisions rather than just picking the strongest individual cards.

walkthrough.title: Uramaki Race Strategy
walkthrough.text: Uramaki is unique because it scores immediately when you hit ten icons, not at round end. The first player to ten gets eight points, second gets five, third gets two. If nobody reaches ten, the highest total at round end takes first. Race hard early or let others burn picks while you score elsewhere.

walkthrough.title: Spoon and Menu Power Plays
walkthrough.text: Spoon lets you name a card type and take it from the first opponent on your left who has one. This is devastating when you need one specific card to complete a high-value set. Menu lets you draw four and pick one, effectively giving you the best card from a fresh selection.

walkthrough.title: Takeout Box Timing
walkthrough.text: Takeout Box lets you flip any number of your previously played cards face down; each flipped card is now worth two points. This is powerful late in a round when you have low-value cards like incomplete sets. Flip worthless single tempura or sashimi into two points each.

walkthrough.title: Dessert Economy Math
walkthrough.text: Desserts enter the deck in decreasing amounts each round. Round one has the most desserts available, making it the easiest time to collect them. If you plan to compete for most desserts, start in round one. Waiting until round three means fewer desserts and more competition.

walkthrough.title: Edamame in Large Groups
walkthrough.text: Edamame scores one point for each opponent who also has edamame. In a six to eight player game, if five players have edamame, each scores four points per edamame card. This makes edamame extremely strong in large groups but useless in small ones.

walkthrough.title: Priority Number Awareness
walkthrough.text: When multiple effects trigger on the same turn, they resolve by printed priority numbers. This matters when chopsticks, spoon, and other specials interact on the same reveal. Knowing the order lets you sequence your plays optimally, especially in menus with two specials that can combo.

walkthrough.title: Cross-Round Planning
walkthrough.text: Unlike the original Sushi Go!, Party rewards thinking across all three rounds. Your dessert strategy, roll totals, and scoring tile awareness all span the entire game. Plan which rounds you push for points versus which rounds you invest in long-term dessert positioning.

bullets: Choose card types that create interesting interactions
Wasabi+nigiri, soy sauce+diversity, tea+depth

entries.definition: Base scoring cards always in every menu. Egg is one point, salmon is two, squid is three. Tripled by wasabi.
A special card that triples the value of the next nigiri played on it. Worth zero alone.
A roll type showing one, two, or three icons. Most icons at round end scores six (or six/four/two with six to eight players), second scores three.
A roll type. Most temaki icons at round end scores four points. Fewest loses four (except in two-player games).
A roll type with an immediate race. First to ten icons scores eight, second scores five, third scores two. If nobody reaches ten, score at round end.
An appetizer. Pair of two scores five points. Single scores zero.
An appetizer. Set of three scores ten points. Fewer than three scores zero.
An appetizer with scaling points. One is one, two is three, three is six, four is ten, five or more is fifteen.
An appetizer. One eel scores negative three points. Two or more eels scores seven points.
An appetizer. One tofu scores two, two scores six, three or more scores zero.
An appetizer with four shapes. Score by variety: one shape is one, two different is four, three different is nine, four different is sixteen.
An appetizer scoring one point per opponent who also has edamame. Cannot be used in two-player games.
An appetizer worth three points, but discarded if any other player plays miso soup on the same turn.
A special allowing you to play two cards on a future turn, returning chopsticks to the passing hand.
A special allowing you to name a card type. The first player to your left with that card gives it to you and takes the spoon. Cannot be used with two players.
A special allowing you to draw four cards from the deck, play one, and return the rest. Cannot be used with seven or eight players.
A special that copies the point value of the card it is played alongside. Cannot be used with seven or eight players.
A special that lets you flip any number of previously played cards face down. Each flipped card scores two points.
A special scoring four points if you have the most different-colored card backs among all players.
A special scoring one point per card in your largest same-type set at round end.
A dessert scored at game end. Most pudding scores six, fewest loses six. In two-player, no fewest penalty.
A dessert scored at game end. Each set of exactly four scores twelve points.
A dessert scored at game end by type variety: watermelon, orange, pineapple. Score based on quantity of each fruit type collected.
A reference tile placed on the board showing the scoring rules for each active card type in the current game.
A small number printed on cards with simultaneous effects. Resolve effects from lowest to highest.
